text: A plaintiff from State A sues a defendant from State B in federal court for breach of contract. The amount in controversy is $60,000. The defendant moves to dismiss for lack of subject matter jurisdiction. How should the court rule?
option_a: Grant the motion because the amount in controversy does not exceed $75,000
option_b: Deny the motion because diversity jurisdiction exists
option_c: Grant the motion because contract claims must be filed in state court
option_d: Deny the motion because federal question jurisdiction applies
option_e: Grant the motion because the plaintiff failed to join a necessary party

text: A driver from California causes an accident in Nevada. The injured pedestrian, a Nevada resident, files suit in California state court. The driver moves to dismiss for improper venue. What is the most likely outcome?
option_a: The motion is granted because the accident occurred in Nevada
option_b: The motion is denied because the driver is domiciled in California
option_c: The motion is granted because the pedestrian should sue where the injury occurred
option_d: The motion is denied because personal jurisdiction and venue are the same analysis
option_e: The motion is granted because the pedestrian lacks standing in California

text: After filing an answer, the defendant discovers new evidence that the plaintiff committed fraud in obtaining the contract at issue. The defendant wants to add a counterclaim for fraud. What procedural step must the defendant take?
option_a: File a new, separate lawsuit for the fraud claim
option_b: Seek leave of court to amend the answer to add the counterclaim
option_c: File a cross-claim against the plaintiff
option_d: Move for summary judgment on the fraud issue

text: During discovery, the plaintiff requests all internal emails from the defendant company related to the product defect. The defendant objects, claiming attorney-client privilege for 200 of the 5,000 responsive emails. What should the defendant do to properly assert the privilege?
option_a: Simply refuse to produce any emails
option_b: Produce a privilege log describing each withheld document
option_c: File a motion to quash the discovery request entirely
option_d: Produce all emails and later move to strike privileged ones
option_e: Assert a blanket privilege over all company communications

text: A class action lawsuit is filed on behalf of 10,000 consumers who purchased a defective product. The named plaintiff seeks class certification under Rule 23(b)(3). Which of the following is NOT a requirement for certification?
option_b: Commonality of questions of law or fact
option_c: Typicality of the named plaintiff's claims
option_d: Unanimity among class members on the desired remedy
option_e: Adequacy of the named plaintiff as class representative

text: The plaintiff serves the complaint on the defendant by leaving a copy with the defendant's 15-year-old child at the defendant's home. The defendant moves to dismiss for insufficient service of process. How should the court rule?
option_a: Grant the motion because minors cannot accept service
option_b: Deny the motion because service on a person of suitable age at the dwelling is proper
option_c: Grant the motion because only personal delivery to the defendant is valid
option_d: Deny the motion because any family member can accept service
option_e: Grant the motion because service must be made at the defendant's workplace

text: After a jury verdict in favor of the plaintiff, the defendant moves for judgment as a matter of law under Rule 50(b). The defendant had not made a Rule 50(a) motion during trial. What is the consequence?
option_a: The court may still grant the motion in the interest of justice
option_b: The motion is procedurally barred for failure to preserve the issue
option_c: The court must grant the motion if the evidence was insufficient
option_d: The defendant can cure the deficiency by filing a motion for new trial
option_e: The failure to file Rule 50(a) has no effect on Rule 50(b)

text: Two plaintiffs with related but distinct claims against the same defendant want to join their claims in a single lawsuit. Plaintiff A has a contract claim and Plaintiff B has a tort claim, both arising from the same construction project. Is joinder proper under Rule 20?
option_a: No, because the claims arise under different legal theories
option_b: Yes, because the claims arise from the same transaction or occurrence and share common questions
option_c: No, because joinder requires identical legal theories
option_d: Yes, but only if both plaintiffs consent to binding arbitration
option_e: No, because contract and tort claims must always be brought separately

text: The court enters a final judgment dismissing the plaintiff's claims. The plaintiff wants to appeal but is concerned about the 30-day deadline. The plaintiff's attorney was hospitalized for two weeks after the judgment was entered. Can the plaintiff obtain an extension of the appeal deadline?
option_a: Yes, the court has unlimited discretion to extend appeal deadlines
option_b: No, the appeal deadline is jurisdictional and cannot be extended for any reason
option_c: Yes, the court may extend the time for good cause shown, within limits
option_d: No, only the appellate court can grant such extensions
option_e: Yes, but only if the opposing party consents to the extension

text: A federal court in the Eastern District of Virginia transfers a case to the Southern District of New York under 28 U.S.C. 1404(a) for convenience of parties and witnesses. Which state's substantive law should the transferee court apply?
option_a: New York law, because the case is now in a New York court
option_b: Virginia law, as the transferor court's choice of law rules follow the case
option_c: Federal common law, because the case is in federal court
option_d: Whichever state's law is more favorable to the plaintiff
option_e: The court applies no state law after transfer
